title: Schizophrenia: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Identify key diagnostic features of Schizophrenia
Apply clinical reasoning to Schizophrenia management

sections.title: Overview of Schizophrenia
sections.content:
**Schizophrenia**

Positive vs negative symptoms, diagnostic criteria, first-line antipsychotics, clozapine for treatment-resistant

**Key Points:**
• Positive symptoms: hallucinations (auditory most common), delusions, disorganized speech/behavior
• Negative symptoms: flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, social withdrawal (harder to treat)
• Diagnosis: ≥2 symptoms for ≥6 months (≥1 must be hallucinations, delusions, or disorganized speech)

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Positive symptoms: hallucinations (auditory most common), delusions, disorganized speech/behavior
• Negative symptoms: flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, social withdrawal (harder to treat)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Diagnosis: ≥2 symptoms for ≥6 months (≥1 must be hallucinations, delusions, or disorganized speech)
• First-line: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, aripiprazole)
• Clozapine: reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia; requires ANC monitoring (agranulocytosis risk)

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Schizophrenia:**

• Diagnosis: ≥2 symptoms for ≥6 months (≥1 must be hallucinations, delusions, or disorganized speech)
• First-line: second-generation antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, aripiprazole)
• Clozapine: reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia; requires ANC monitoring (agranulocytosis risk)

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Schizophrenia?

options.text: Positive symptoms: hallucinations (auditory most common), delusions, disorganized speech/behavior
options.explanation: Positive symptoms: hallucinations (auditory most common), delusions, disorganized speech/behavior

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Schizophrenia?

options.text: Clozapine: reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia; requires ANC monitoring (agranulocytosis risk)
options.explanation: Clozapine: reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia; requires ANC monitoring (agranulocytosis risk)

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Schizophrenia. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Negative symptoms: flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, social withdrawal (harder to treat)
options.explanation: Negative symptoms: flat affect, alogia, avolition, anhedonia, social withdrawal (harder to treat)

mcqs.stem: A 22-year-old college student presents with 5 days of decreased sleep (2 hours/night with no fatigue), pressured speech, grandiose plans to start three companies, and $15,000 in impulsive purchases. Which diagnosis and initial treatment are most appropriate?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder, manic episode — start lithium or valproate plus an atypical antipsychotic
options.explanation: This meets DSM-5 criteria for a manic episode: ≥1 week of elevated/expansive mood with ≥3 symptoms (decreased need for sleep, pressured speech, grandiosity, impulsive spending). Acute mania treatment per APA guidelines includes mood stabilizer (lithium or valproate) plus atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, quetiapine, or risperidone) for rapid symptom control.

options.text: Bipolar II disorder, hypomanic episode — start an SSRI
options.explanation: Hypomania by definition does NOT cause marked impairment or require hospitalization. $15,000 in impulsive purchases and severe functional impairment indicate full mania (Bipolar I). SSRIs can trigger or worsen mania and are not first-line.

options.text: ADHD with comorbid anxiety — start stimulant medication
options.explanation: While ADHD can present with impulsivity and pressured speech, the episodic nature, decreased need for sleep without fatigue, and grandiosity are characteristic of mania. Stimulants can worsen manic symptoms.

options.text: Substance-induced mood disorder — observe and toxicology screen only
options.explanation: While substance use should always be ruled out, a 5-day sustained presentation with classic manic symptoms should not be attributed to substances without evidence. Treatment should not be delayed pending a negative tox screen.
